TextENGNew: I walked in the door and the house was already taut with tension.
Not exactly an unusual state of affairs around here.
Whenever Dad hits a wall with a new painting, the whole house feels it.
He stomps around with his arms crossed, groaning under his breath.
Mom pulled me aside and murmured in my ear.
"Welcome home, Keiichi. Dad's having a rough time, so just give him some space, okay?"
"Another creative block?"
"It's just that the deadlines are stacking up this time. Your father needs time to recharge, otherwise he just can't function."
The whole family survives off Dad's paintings. If his ideas dry up, the Maebaras are finished.
(...We've been eating fine up to now. ...Wait—could Dad actually be... some kind of master painter??)
"Wouldn't it be easier to get ideas by going for a walk outside instead of pacing around the house?"
"The theme this time is 'domestic space.' It seems he wants to use this house itself as the motif."
...What an incredibly inconvenient motif.
"...Keiichi!! Clean up your room for once!! It's an embarrassment!"
"S-Sorry! I'll get to it later!"
Oh come on! Please, not my room—!
"Keiichi, there's still time before dinner. Go take a walk outside or something."
Dad's moods don't last.
The moment an idea hits him, his whole demeanor flips and he starts humming to himself.
Until then, a walk to keep out of his way wasn't the worst idea.
"Yeah, I suppose I will."
A walk—sure, but where exactly?
I just needed to kill a little time.
I swung a leg over my bike and thought about where to go.
I would have liked to browse at the bookstore, but that was an hour by bike.
It'd be dark by the time I got back, so I didn't want to bother unless I had a reason.
Hinamizawa at night gives me the creeps, honestly.

Mion or Satoko can never find out about that though...
I smiled a little, ruefully.
So—what now?
Oh, right.
Rena was probably still at her mountain of treasure—the old dam construction site.
She'd probably been struggling to dig out the Randy-kun doll this whole time.
Helping her out wouldn't be a bad move.
"...She might return the favor at next club activities."
With a touch of calculated self-interest, I pointed my bike toward the dam site.
Tomitake-san might be there too.
The one person who knew anything about the incident that both Rena and Mion had flatly denied knowing.
If I ran into him again, I wanted to hear more.
...To ask him point-blank: "Was there really a murder in Hinamizawa?"
That was my real agenda.
I spotted Rena struggling on the slope of the garbage pile.
The Randy-kun doll looked thoroughly wedged in.
There was no way she was going to dig it out by herself.
Seeing no sign of Tomitake-san, I made my way carefully down the unsteady slope.
"Hey, Rena. Working hard out here, I see."
"Wh—Keiichi-kun!"
"What are you doing all the way out here?"
All the way out here, huh? At least she's self-aware.
"Emergency response unit, on the scene! Where is the injured party?!"
"Huh?! Huh?! Injured—? Huh?!"
"Reports of a Randy-kun doll buried alive in a garbage heap—!"
"Huh? ...Oh—ohh, that. You scared me... Keiichi-kun, don't startle me like that."
"Just kidding. I figured you might be struggling alone out here, so I came to help."
"...Eh? ...You came... for me...?"
Daily quota: fulfilled.
A day without seeing Rena blush at least once is nutritionally incomplete.
"I'm joking! Now you're making me embarrassed, too!"
"...So... wait? Which part was the joke? Which part was the joke!?"
I left her confusion to its own devices.
"Alright, move aside. Now—where is Randy-kun?"
"Ah, sorry! ...Can you see him through that gap?"
"He's... actually buried in there, isn't he...!!"
Randy-kun lay on his side, a tangle of lumber and building material caged around him like a cell.
According to Rena, it hadn't looked like this yesterday.
Apparently another illegal dumper had come through overnight and buried him further.
"...You were planning to clear all of this by yourself...?! With those arms...?!"
With arms as slender as hers, that simply wasn't happening.
"...But... Randy-kun is so cute... The one at the store has a chain on him, but this one... I could actually take this one home..."
If Rena gave up on this Randy-kun, she'd probably start plotting to steal the display model from the shop...
As Rena's self-appointed guardian, I absolutely could not let her go down the path of crime.
"Get out of the way. I'll handle it."
Rena blushed again, but I let it pass this time.
The pile was enormous.
And the longer we left it, the more likely another truck would come dump more.
If it got buried any deeper, there'd be no hope.
"Keiichi-kun, I can help too. Let me help."
"You'd just be in the way—stand back! Here we goooo!!"
Wrenching out debris, snapping pieces, hurling them aside.
I was covered in sweat and grime in no time.
Under the clear sky, lumber, timber, plywood—each one traced a clean arc as I sent it flying.
Damn it all!! No matter how much I cleared, there was always more!!
Randy-kun was right there, visible—so close!!
I'd made such a big show of it in front of Rena, and now I was barely making progress. The frustration was building.
"...If I'm really going to do this properly, I might need an axe or a saw or something...!"
"That's enough, Keiichi-kun...! You're drenched in sweat. ...You don't have to push yourself so hard..."
"I'm doing this for you. Don't worry about it."
Rena sputtered and went beet red.
Ah, whoops... I'd meant to say I was doing it to keep her out of trouble with the law...
Oh well.
I collapsed spread-eagle onto the grassy slope.
"Alright, I need a break...! This thing is... seriously formidable...!!"
"I'm so sorry... You're completely drenched..."
Rena dabbed her handkerchief against my forehead.
It felt nice.
"Oh, um—wait here a minute! My house is nearby. I'll bring some barley tea!"
She left her handkerchief on my forehead and took off at a sprint.
So then I...
It was only until Dad was in a better mood, anyway. I just needed to kill a little time—that was all.
That was fine, but—where exactly should I go?
With nowhere particular in mind, I ended up pedaling my bike toward the shopping district.
...Dad's line of work really does make my life complicated.
Unstable income, home all day, and the moment he's frustrated, everyone has to tiptoe around him. From a kid's perspective, it's a real nuisance.
Times like this, I find myself thinking: I am never going to be an artist. Not in a million years.
I was meandering along on my bike, half-griping to myself, when I noticed a familiar figure outside the greengrocer's.
Was that Satoko? She seemed to be having some trouble deciding between vegetables.
Chance! A moth to a flame, and I'd just found the perfect way to kill time.
I crept up silently behind her.
"Um... this one... is broccoli... isn't it."
She was staring holes through a head of cauliflower.
"Broccoli...?"
Satoko glanced at the actual broccoli sitting right beside it.
A textbook setup. If not now, when?
She sensed someone behind her and spun around with a start.
"K-Keiichi-san?! What are you doing here?!"
"Hey there. I live in Hinamizawa too, you know."
"W-Well, yes, that's true, but..."
As she spoke, she casually returned the cauliflower to the shelf.
"...Aren't you buying that broccoli?"
"Eh... well. Not the broccoli today. What about you, Keiichi-san—what did you come to buy?"
"Me? Lettuce, maybe."
I picked up a head of cabbage as I said this.
"Hmm... lettuce..."
"It's on sale. Satoko, why not get some lettuce too? And hey—cauliflower, look!"
I dropped a cabbage and a head of broccoli into Satoko's basket.
"Th-Thank you..."
I bit back a grin while Satoko stared at me with bewildered confusion.
"W-W-What is it...?"
"Nothing. How about asparagus?"
I put the okra into the basket as I said it.
Satoko, who had been giving me a suspicious look, went rigid—then flushed crimson.
"Keiichi-san! That's okra!"
"Ha! Exactly right! How about the rest of them—you got all the jokes, didn't you?!"
changeReason: Old translation has a typo 'Satsuko' instead of the correct character name 'Satoko.'

TextENGNew: "J-Jokes...?! O-Of course!"
Satoko began putting the items back on the shelf one by one.
Only one thing was left in the basket: broccoli.
"That's what you actually came here for, isn't it?"
Satoko stared at the broccoli.
"Cauliflower I'm not buying; broccoli I am. That about sums it up. So—which one is this?"
A deep silence settled around them. ...Hey, come on, you're overthinking this, Satoko!
Before long, the shopkeeper came shuffling out from the back, impatient.
"Are you buying that broccoli or not?"
"Broccoli! Of course I'm buying it!"
I couldn't stop laughing at her flustered panic. Well done surviving that, Satoko.
changeReason: Old translation is garbled ('Thanks her for saving your life...... Satoko!'); new correctly renders the meaning as 'Well done surviving that, Satoko,' fixing a broken sentence.

TextENGNew: And then—
Jingle jingle jingle~!
Satoko hurriedly yanked out her wallet, and coins scattered across the pavement. There weren't many judging by the sounds, but a few had rolled a fair distance from her feet.
"Come on, Satoko—try to stay calm when you're paying!"
"I-I know that! Um, where..."
She squatted down in a hurry to collect them. I crouched alongside her. A few had rolled all the way to the feet of passersby.
But.
The passersby stopped in front of coins that had rolled to their feet, reached to pick them up—and then looked at the face of the person who had dropped them.
That was the first time I felt it. As soon as they saw it was Satoko, they withdrew their hands and walked on.
Why wouldn't they pick them up...?
I glanced back at the greengrocer's staff. The clerk was paying absolutely no attention, not even looking at Satoko scrambling to collect her coins.
Come to think of it, had the villagers always been this cold toward Satoko? Or was that just my imagination...?
Watching her scrabble after her coins, I felt a sharp pang of guilt for all the teasing I'd done.
The cicadas' song slowly cooled the air.
Once I was sure Rena was gone, I got up and made my way toward what I'd spotted earlier.
It was a pile of old newspapers and weekly magazines bound with string.
Unless I'd been mistaken... I was fairly sure they'd been stacked somewhere around here...
There they were.
Bundles of tabloid weeklies—the less reputable kind.
Back issues from the past several years, stacked in order.
"...A nasty piece of work, wasn't it. ...And they still haven't found it, have they?"
If what Tomitake-san had said was accurate, something in here would confirm there had been a murder. ...And there was still something out there—unfound.
These are grim times. Incidents like this never seem to stop.
And there's always a large crowd of people drawn to them, feeding on the morbid details.
So it had to be in here. Somewhere.
I worked quickly, peeling apart rain-stuck pages to check the tables of contents.
Not knowing when it happened made this harder.
I didn't know the perpetrators or the victim.
All I knew was that it had happened here.
I looked up periodically to check whether Rena had come back.
I didn't want her to find me rifling through sleazy tabloids—but it wasn't only that.
Rena had said she didn't know. Mion had said she didn't know.
But it had happened. No question.
...Unless Tomitake-san was lying.
If Rena and Mion had both just said, "Yeah, there was something like that," maybe my strange obsession would have settled down on its own.
An "incident" neither Rena nor Mion could bring themselves to speak of.
Trying to unearth something they'd covered up out of consideration for me...
The thought sat in my chest like a low, ugly guilt.
"HINAMIZAWA DAM WORKER BRUTALITY—DISMEMBERMENT MURDER!!"
changeReason: Old translates 凶行 as 'LYNCHING'; new correctly renders it as 'BRUTALITY.' The Japanese 凶行 means atrocity/brutality, not lynching.

TextENGNew: ...There it was!
The feature article was in the back, but it looked like there was a photograph in the color pages up front.
The featured pages were stuck together and wouldn't open easily.
Rena could be back any moment.
Feeling the pressure, I gave up on that and opened the photo spread instead.
Investigators carrying a body bag, reporters surrounding them in a blaze of camera flashes.
The image was dark and hard to make out, but the white headlines were unmistakable.

"A NIGHTMARE AT THE HINAMIZAWA DAM—REPRISAL, DISMEMBERMENT MURDER!"
"Victim was the site foreman"
"Daily abuse of his workers by the aggressor..."
"Frustrations finally snap—the foreman was found in unspeakably gruesome condition..."
...It happened. ...It really, truly happened.
The details seemed to begin on the next page.
...Without hesitation, I turned it.
...On it was...
"The perpetrators beat the victim to death with extreme brutality, then used a hatchet to divide the body into six pieces—"
changeReason: Old translation adds details not in the original Japanese ('the head, arms, legs, and torso'); new translation stays closer to the source with 'divide the body into six pieces—' without inventing the specific body parts.

TextENGNew: Even just the headlines said enough... This was... simply too horrific.
Normally, when someone takes revenge, it's hitting and kicking, isn't it...?
Axes, pickaxes, hatchets? This went far beyond the word "retaliation."
changeReason: Old says 'Dismemberment with hatchets and pickaxes?'; new says 'Hatchets, pickaxes, axes?' The Japanese lists 斧 (axe), つるはし (pickaxe), and 鉈 (hatchet), and the old translation omits 'axes' while the new includes all three.

TextENGNew: Outright slaughter. Brutal murder...
Multiple people. Ganging up together.
...With axes. ...With pickaxes. ...With a hatchet.
"AaAaAaAaAaAh!!!"
"Eek! S-Sorry...!!! Did I startle you? Did I startle you?!"
Rena had been startled by my shout and dropped the hatchet she'd been carrying into the grass.
"Keiichi-kun, you mentioned earlier that an axe would be useful, right?! S-So... I went and fetched a hatchet from the shed for you...!!"
Rena kept babbling apologies and explanations in a fluster.
Apparently I'd been giving her quite the intense look.
"S-Sorry... that was a bit of an overreaction on my part."
"N-No, it's... I-I'm sorry too! Sorry!"
...The sun would be setting soon.
I was exhausted enough; leaving the rest for tomorrow seemed reasonable.
"That last beam isn't going to budge without that hatchet. And you went to all the trouble of bringing it... I'll borrow it tomorrow, alright?"
"...Okay."
"Why the long face? Tomorrow we'll finally dig Randy-kun out, won't we?!"
"That's true. ...Ahahaha! I really want to take Randy-kun home soon~!"
We both knew there was little point in the apologies going any further.
We drank the barley tea Rena had brought, wiped down the sweat that had gone cold on our skin, and started for home.
The tabloid hidden under my jacket weighed on my conscience.
The higurashi's song cooled the air slowly.
While Rena was gone, I'd looked around to see if there was anywhere else I could excavate.
*rustle*...
What? Rena? Something behind me—I turned around. And then—
"Whoa!"
Instead of Rena, it was a tanned, broad-shouldered man—Tomitake-san.
changeReason: Old translation says 'The gaze that I had assumed was Rena's was on a tanned, well-built man named Tomitake'; new says 'Instead of Rena, it was a tanned, broad-shouldered man—Tomitake-san.' Old implies Tomitake was staring at Keiichi; new correctly identifies Tomitake as the person Keiichi sees.

TextENGNew: I was genuinely startled. I'd half-expected Rena to have transformed into some kind of bear-woman... which, okay, that thought speaks for itself.
"Sorry, sorry. Did I startle you again?"
Tomitake-san walked over, clearly amused.
I forced a somewhat awkward laugh, trying to look calmer than I felt.
"You always appear out of nowhere. Though I'll admit, this time I was half-expecting someone else, so I startled myself a bit too..."
"Half-expecting someone else?"
"Never mind."
No matter how easy-going Tomitake-san seemed, there was no way I could tell him I'd expected a bear-woman.
changeReason: Old translation has Tomitake as subject ('he couldn't say that she looked like a bear'); new correctly has Keiichi as subject ('there was no way I could tell him I'd expected a bear-woman'), fixing a subject error.

TextENGNew: "Get any good shots?"
"Hm? Yeah, pretty good ones. No matter how many times I come here, it never gets old. You forget all the noise of the city and feel genuinely at ease."
"That's true... really."
"...Being here now, it's almost hard to believe that incident ever happened."
changeReason: Old translation drops the 'here/this place' element; new preserves it ('Being here now, it's almost hard to believe'). More importantly, old omits context while new conveys Tomitake's full thought including the setting.

TextENGNew: "Excuse me—what incident?"
"Hm? ...Oh, you mean you... don't know?"
"Ah, I only just moved here..."
"Is that so?..."

He murmured something under his breath and sighed faintly, as if regretting having spoken. From his expression, I sensed that whatever "incident" he'd mentioned was something that sat poorly against the peaceful surroundings.
But back then...
"There wasn't."
Mion's crisp words came back to me. At the time she'd said nothing had happened during the dam construction. And yet without doubt, something had.
...Unless Tomitake-san was lying.
If Mion had just said, "Yeah, there was something," maybe my curiosity would have settled.
The "incident" Mion had deliberately chosen not to speak of.
Somehow it felt like they were concealing it out of consideration for me, and yet... with Rena away, I wanted to hear it now.
"So you really didn't know about it?"
"No. Not at all."
"I see... It's not a pleasant story, but do you mind?"
"I don't mind."
I said it with a decisiveness that surprised even me. I must have been more troubled by the incident than I'd consciously realized.
"It's a nightmare, what happened at the Hinamizawa Dam. ...A dismemberment murder."
"A dismemberment... murder?!"
changeReason: Old translates 殺人 as 'Incident'; new correctly translates it as 'murder.' 殺人 specifically means homicide/murder.

TextENGNew: "That's right. The victim was the site foreman. Apparently he treated his workers brutally on a daily basis..."
"The workers beat the victim to death in a savage attack. And then used an axe to divide the body into six pieces, they say."
How horrific... What a case.
When someone settles a grudge, it's usually punching or kicking, isn't it?
Hatchets, pickaxes, axes? You can't call this by the same word—it's something else entirely.
Rather than the explosion of pent-up frustration, it was... something dark that had long since left reason behind.
Multiple people... closing in together... with a hatchet... with pickaxes... with axes...
"It made the color photo spreads of the weekly magazines. That's how famous it was."
"But... no one here has ever said a word to me about it..."
"They're looking out for you. You've only just arrived, and I imagine they don't want you to end up hating this village because of some ugly old story."
"Even so..."
I couldn't quite accept that. Everyone around me behaved as though they were deliberately keeping it from me.
"Sorry to have told you something unpleasant. But it really is a lovely village at heart! Take care!"
Tomitake-san wrapped things up cheerfully and disappeared just like that.
At heart... I wasn't convinced.
"AaAaAaAaAaAh!!!"
"Eek! S-Sorry...!!! Did I startle you? Did I startle you?!"
Rena had been startled by my shout and dropped the hatchet she'd been holding into the grass.
"Keiichi-kun, you mentioned earlier that a hatchet would be handy, right?! S-So... I went and got one for you from the shed...!!"
Rena kept babbling apologies and explanations in a fluster.
Apparently I'd been giving her quite the intense look.
"S-Sorry... that was a bit of an overreaction on my part."
"N-No, don't—I-I'm the one who's sorry! Sorry!"
...The sun would be setting soon.
I was exhausted enough; leaving the rest for tomorrow seemed reasonable.
"Hey, Rena... "
"Hm? What is it? What is it?"
changeReason: Old translation has a typo 'y ou' with an erroneous space; new translation corrects it to 'you.'

TextENGNew: Looking at Rena's face, Tomitake-san's words drifted back to me. ...'They're looking out for you.'
"Nothing, never mind."
"Now you've got me curious~ What happened? What happened?"
I'd decided not to push her about the incident. Not until the day she chose to tell me herself.
"Nothing. ...I was just thinking Rena looks really cute today, that's all."
"...Hau... Th-That's kind of weird to say..."
changeReason: Old translates はぅ as 'Huh'; new correctly renders it as 'Hau,' Rena's signature exclamation, preserving character voice.

TextENGNew: I felt a small stab of guilt, but making Rena blush was something I could do as many times a day as presented itself.
We drank the barley tea she'd brought, wiped off the sweat that had gone cold on our skin, and started back home.
Walking beside Rena, who knew nothing of what I'd discovered in her absence, I felt quietly guilty.
Once Satoko had finished her shopping, she loaded the vegetables into her bicycle basket.
"Now then, Keiichi-san—hurry up and get yourself home!"
Maybe she was still thinking about what had happened earlier—she was putting on more cheer than usual, and the effort of it made her look a little fragile, a little sad.
What was the right thing to say to Satoko at a time like this? The words wouldn't come. I just got on my bike.
"Well then, Keiichi-san. ...Until next time..."
"Yeah. ...Take care on your way home."
I was about to go. But Satoko wasn't leaving.
"Ugh! ...Wh-What is wrong with this basket?!"
CLUNK! CLUNK! The basket on Satoko's bike was rocking and wobbling.
The bracket holding the basket had come loose. What should have been a sturdy container for her groceries was now drooping at a helpless angle.
"Ugh!"
She rocked the basket with a look like she was about to cry.
It was hard to watch. As if things weren't already bad enough.
So I stopped teasing for once and spoke up.
"...Satoko. I'll carry it to your place."
"I-I'm fine!"
"Just give it here."
I picked up the bag of vegetables and hung it from my own handlebars.
"Th-Thank you... But please—don't go getting any stupid ideas about making off with the vegetables!"
"What are you talking about? I was thinking of throwing in a few extra carrots and squash as a bonus. Sound good?"
"Hmph! In that case, I'd prefer meat, thank you very much! A prime Matsuzaka cut with the finest marbling, if you please!"
She shot back with a grin, playing right into my provocation. If she could banter like that, she must have found a little of her energy again... I felt something warm stir inside me as I watched her.
changeReason: Old translation ends with garbled text ('It's a great way to make sure you're getting the most out of your time with us'); new correctly conveys 'I felt something warm stir inside me as I watched her,' matching the Japanese ふと嬉しさがこみ上げてきた.

TextENGNew: "W-What are you smiling at?!"
She peered at my face as we pedaled side by side.
"Nothing. ...Hey, watch where you're looking or you'll crash."
"You're the one who should watch out, Keiichi-san! —Oh."
Just as she said it, Satoko's brakes screeched and she stopped dead. For a moment I thought something was wrong with her bike, but that wasn't it.
I followed her gaze. It landed on the display window of a toy shop.
"Is there something you want?"
Lined up in the window were board games, dolls, stuffed animals—the usual contents of any toy store. What was Satoko looking at...?
"...No. It's nothing."
Satoko started pedaling again, her broken basket rattling and clanking. I quickly followed.
Two bicycles moved side by side, slowly, against the sunset.
It wasn't long before Satoko and Rika-chan's house came into view.
As we rode along talking and laughing about nothing in particular, the stiffness in Satoko's profile eased, little by little, into something brighter and more relaxed...
Seeing her natural smile come back gave me a quiet sense of relief.
"...Well then, Keiichi-san—see you tomorrow~"
After finishing her shopping, Satoko managed a smile—a bit stilted—and quickly got on her bike.
"Oh, yeah... See you."
I watched her pedal away without any particular urgency. The slender line of her back grew smaller and fainter until she was just an indistinct outline.
We'd parted without clearing the air between us... A leftover awkwardness hung over the empty street.
It was still too early to go home. I pushed my bike along and wandered aimlessly down the shopping street.
The cold looks the villagers had given Satoko had lodged in a corner of my mind, and nothing I saw managed to lift the weight.
I was browsing the storefronts with that hollow feeling when I caught sight of a familiar silhouette.
Hmm...? That person looks like...
I went straight toward him and pulled up my bike to get a closer look.
"Tomitake-san, I thought so."
"Oh, Keiichi-kun."
Tomitake-san gave a small, surprised look.
"What a coincidence running into you here. Out shopping?"
It seemed like every time we crossed paths, it was by sheer accident... well, whatever.
"Ah, yeah..."
I suddenly thought of Rika-chan.
Now that I thought about it, the last time I'd run into Tomitake-san, Rika-chan had been with me. All the village elders had flocked to her, and a crowd had formed around her in no time...

Thinking back to the scene outside the greengrocer's earlier, I felt a pang of sympathy for Satoko. Satoko and Rika-chan—why did people react so differently to the two of them? Was there some special reason Rika-chan was treated so differently...?
...Come to think of it, hadn't Tomitake-san said something back then about Rika-chan and Oyashiro-sama...?
Right. He definitely had. I'd tried to ask him more, but Rika-chan had cut the conversation off.
This might be my chance to ask...
I worked up the nerve to bring it up.
"...I'd like to know more about Oyashiro-sama."
"Oyashiro-sama...?"
Tomitake-san's expression shifted, as though weighing something. I waited for what came next.

"To be honest, I don't know as much about Oyashiro-sama as the people who actually live here. The villagers seem to have a very particular relationship with the deity—something that goes beyond what an outsider like me could speak to."
"A... particular relationship...?"
"Ah, I don't know that much about it, but..."
With that caveat, Tomitake-san began to speak, picking his words carefully.
"Put simply, Oyashiro-sama is the guardian deity of Hinamizawa."
"It would be no exaggeration to say that the villagers' faith runs deep—that their sense of unity revolves around Oyashiro-sama."
"When they tried to build a dam in Hinamizawa, the villagers stood as one. Their faith in Oyashiro-sama was what held them together."
"The dam..."

Now that he mentioned it, I thought I'd heard something about this from Mion—that the residents of Hinamizawa had banded together to fight the officials who wanted to drown the village under the reservoir.
"Yes... Even so, no one anticipated what that eventually led to..."
At that, Tomitake-san's expression clouded.
"An incident? ...What happened?"
I raised an eyebrow, and Tomitake-san hesitated briefly before speaking.
"It's not a pleasant story..."
I nodded, and swallowed.
"A nightmare that took place at the Hinamizawa Dam. ...A dismemberment murder."
"A dismemberment... murder?!"
"...The victim was the dam construction foreman. I only know what I've heard secondhand, but apparently the foreman had been mistreating his workers on a regular basis."
"One day, the pent-up workers turned on the foreman and beat him to death. And then, according to what I've heard, used an axe to divide the body into six pieces..."
I was stunned.
What a horrific thing to have happened...!
When someone takes revenge for a grudge, it's usually punching and kicking, isn't it?
Hatchets, pickaxes, axes? You couldn't use the same word for something like this—it had gone far beyond ordinary.
"But... no one here has ever said a single word to me about it..."
"They're looking out for you. You've only just arrived, and I imagine they don't want you to end up hating this village because of some ugly old story."
"Even so..."
"Sorry to have told you something unpleasant. Well, I have some things to take care of. Take care!"
Tomitake-san closed the conversation with a bright farewell and vanished into the crowd before I could say another word.
Why was everyone keeping this from me? Was there some meaning to hiding it...? Maybe I really didn't know anything about this village at all.
Left alone on the shopping street, I stood there wrapped in a low, creeping unease.
The cicadas' voices echoed in from the forest, drifting to my ears.
Was there something in this village that it was dangerous for me to know...?
